title: Somatic Therapy for Self-Sabotage: When You Keep Getting in Your Own Way
excerpt: Discover how somatic therapy reveals why you self-sabotage and how body-based healing rewires patterns that keep you stuck.
body:
Understanding Self-Sabotage Through the Body

Self-sabotage is a frustrating experience of getting in your own way, often without clear reason. You set goals or make plans, but something inside halts your progress. You might procrastinate, engage in destructive behaviors, or freeze at critical moments. Many who have tried talk therapy find themselves stuck because self-sabotage isn’t just a mental or behavioral issue—it’s deeply rooted in the body’s survival systems.

Somatic therapy approaches self-sabotage by tuning into the body’s sensations, patterns, and implicit memories. This method is grounded in the work of pioneers like Peter Levine, Bessel van der Kolk, Stephen Porges, and Pat Ogden, who demonstrate that trauma and dysregulation live in the nervous system and manifest as self-defeating behaviors.

Why Traditional Therapy May Miss the Mark

Traditional talk therapy tends to focus on thoughts and emotions, which are important but often only the tip of the iceberg. When self-sabotage feels automatic or unconscious, it’s a sign that the nervous system is driving behaviors below the level of conscious awareness. Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score, explains that trauma and stress are stored in the body, not just the brain. Without addressing the body, the stuck patterns often persist.

In other words, if your nervous system is stuck in a survival mode—hyperaroused or shut down—it can trigger the very behaviors that feel like self-sabotage. You’re not really "getting in your own way" intentionally; your biology is responding to perceived threats based on past experiences.

The Nervous System’s Role in Self-Sabotage

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory is crucial for understanding how the autonomic nervous system shapes behavior. According to Porges, our nervous system has three primary states:

  • Ventral Vagal: The social engagement system, where we feel safe, connected, and able to pursue goals.
  • Sympathetic: The fight-or-flight response, mobilizing energy to escape or defend.
  • Dorsal Vagal: The shutdown or freeze response, conserving energy in overwhelming situations.

When you self-sabotage, your nervous system may be stuck in sympathetic arousal (anxiety, hypervigilance) or dorsal vagal shutdown (numbness, dissociation). These states interfere with goal-directed action, making it hard to sustain effort or take risks.

For example, procrastination can be a sympathetic-driven avoidance of perceived threat, while giving up prematurely might be a dorsal vagal shutdown protecting you from overwhelm. Recognizing these states somatically helps you decode why you keep getting in your own way.

How Trauma and Early Experiences Shape Self-Sabotage

Trauma—both big-T and small-t—rewires the nervous system, creating implicit survival strategies that can look like self-sabotage in adulthood. Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy and Peter Levine’s Somatic Experiencing emphasize that trauma is stored in body memories and muscle tension patterns.

Gabor Maté highlights how early relational trauma impacts self-regulation and self-worth, often manifesting as self-destructive patterns later in life. If your nervous system learned early on that safety meant shutting down or fighting, your body will keep defaulting to those patterns when stressed.

Somatic therapy helps bring these unconscious patterns into awareness by tracking bodily sensations, breath, and posture, which are the gateways to unlocking stuck survival responses.

Somatic Tools to Interrupt Self-Sabotage Patterns

Practical somatic tools can help you interrupt self-sabotage before it unfolds. Here are some evidence-based techniques:

  • Body Scan and Mindful Awareness: Following Pat Ogden’s approach, gently noticing sensations in the body without judgment helps you reconnect with your nervous system’s current state.
  • Grounding and Centering Exercises: Using techniques from Deb Dana’s work on the Polyvagal Theory, grounding yourself through feet-on-the-floor sensations or slow rhythmic breathing can shift your nervous system into a more regulated state.
  • Pendulation: Peter Levine’s method of moving attention between areas of tension and relaxation in the body to renegotiate traumatic energy and release stuck survival responses.
  • Resourcing: Building internal safety resources such as imagining a safe place or accessing memories of feeling calm and competent.

These tools aren’t about forcing change but about inviting the nervous system to feel safe enough to let go of old survival habits driving self-sabotage.

Working With Parts: Integrating Internal Conflicts

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model complements somatic therapy by helping you identify and work with different parts of yourself that may be in conflict. For example, a protective part may sabotage your success to avoid vulnerability, while another part wants growth and healing.

Combining somatic awareness with IFS lets you feel the bodily sensations associated with these parts, deepening understanding and compassion toward them. This integration is key to breaking self-sabotage cycles that feel like internal battles.

Why Patience and Compassion Matter

Healing self-sabotage is not about quick fixes or willpower. As Francine Shapiro’s EMDR research shows, reprocessing traumatic memories through body-based methods leads to lasting change, but it takes time. Dan Siegel’s work on integration reminds us that the goal is to build coherent internal narratives and nervous system regulation, not perfection.

Self-sabotage behaviors are survival strategies that once helped you cope. Approaching your nervous system with patience and curiosity rather than judgment creates the conditions for real transformation.

“You’re not really ‘getting in your own way’ intentionally; your biology is responding to perceived threats based on past experiences.” — The Oracle Lover

Getting Started With Somatic Therapy for Self-Sabotage

If you’ve tried talk therapy and still feel stuck, consider exploring somatic therapy approaches. You can start with self-directed practices like body scans, grounding, and mindful movement. Journaling about bodily sensations alongside thoughts and emotions can deepen insight.

Working with a trauma-informed somatic therapist trained in approaches such as Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or EMDR can accelerate healing by providing skilled guidance through the nervous system’s subtle signals.

Ultimately, somatic therapy offers a pathway out of self-sabotage by reconnecting you with your body’s wisdom and capacity for regulation and resilience.

Summary

  • Self-sabotage often stems from nervous system dysregulation rooted in trauma and survival patterns.
  • Traditional therapy can miss these embodied processes driving unconscious behaviors.
  • Somatic therapy uses the body as a gateway to healing by tracking sensations, regulating nervous system states, and renegotiating trauma.
  • Tools like grounding, pendulation, and parts work help interrupt self-sabotage cycles.
  • Patience, compassion, and integration are key to lasting change.

By learning to listen to and regulate your body, you can finally stop getting in your own way and move toward the life you want.

References

  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Penguin Books.
  • Levine, P. A. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. W. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts: Close Encounters with Addiction. North Atlantic Books.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Siegel, D. J. (2012). The Developing Mind: How Relationships and the Brain Interact to Shape Who We Are. Guilford Press.
  • Schwartz, R. C. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.
  • Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy, Third Edition: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: What is somatic therapy and how does it help with self-sabotage?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on the body to address trauma and nervous system dysregulation that underlie self-sabotage, helping you regulate your responses and break unconscious patterns.

faqs.q: Can I practice somatic therapy techniques on my own?
faqs.a: Yes, you can start with simple grounding, breath awareness, and body scanning exercises to increase bodily awareness and nervous system regulation, but working with a trained therapist is beneficial for deeper work.

faqs.q: Why do I keep self-sabotaging even though I want to change?
faqs.a: Self-sabotage is often driven by unconscious nervous system responses rooted in past trauma or stress, which override conscious intentions until the body learns new regulation patterns.

faqs.q: How long does it take to see changes with somatic therapy?
faqs.a: Change varies by person and history, but consistent practice and compassionate self-awareness can lead to shifts in weeks to months, with deeper integration over time.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma is stored in the body and offers insights into somatic healing.

metaTitle: Somatic Therapy for Self-Sabotage: Break the Cycle
metaDescription: Learn how somatic therapy helps you understand and overcome self-sabotage by healing your nervous system and body-based patterns.
author: The Oracle Lover
